sections.instruction: Questions 21-26: Choose the correct letter A, B or C. Questions 27-30: Complete the notes below using NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS for each answer.

questions.content: Before giving his presentation Paul is worried about
options: A choosing what information to use
B not remembering what to say
C organizing the projection equipment

questions.content: Paul’s friends advise him not to
options: A speak about popular periods
B talk at length about art history
C speak too quickly in the presentation

options: A it will be visually pleasing
B it will save some time
C it will keep him focused

questions.content: Paul is going to show famous works of art to
options: A show people that he has good taste
B make people think about what counts as art
C prove that modern art is not as good as classic art

questions.content: Paul wants his presentation to
options: A change personal opinions
B create a big fight
C start an exciting debate

options: A they are skillful
B they paint like children
C they have a sense of freedom

sections.instruction: Questions 31-33: Choose the correct letter A, B or C. Questions 34 and 35: Complete the sentences below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/ OR A NUMBER for each answer. Questions 36-40: Complete the summary below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS for each answer.

questions.content: What does the speaker say about newspapers?
options: A they never report developments in science accurately
B he became interested in his subject because of a newspaper report
C they only report developments in renewable energy technology

questions.content: What significant event took place in 1956?
options: A scientists discovered a new source of energy
B a new type of power station came into service
C Helium was first produced from hydrogen

questions.content: What does the speaker consider to be the main problem with nuclear fusion?
options: A irresponsible scientists make false claims on the subject
B it takes too long to produce the required reaction
C the reaction has not produced usable quantities of energy

questions.content: Temperatures of at least {{BLANK}} degrees are needed for fusion to take place.
Creating the pressure required in a {{BLANK}} is a major technological problem.

In order for nuclear fusion to produce useful quantities of energy isotopes of hydrogen must be brought together at extremely high temperatures and pressures. A number of {{BLANK}} will suspend the fuel so that it does not come into contact with the sides of the {{BLANK}}. Another one will raise the temperature of the fuel pellet to that required to start the {{BLANK}}. Extra energy released in this process will be used to produce {{BLANK}} that can be used to generate electricity. It is believed that the amount of energy that can be produced from {{BLANK}} of fusion fuel is equivalent to that which can be produced from 10000 tonnes of fossil fuel.
